observation_summary: The JsonParserSequence skips a token when switching between concatenated parsers, causing the parser to miss the current token of the second parser.
hypothesis: The original implementation of nextToken() in JsonParserSequence unconditionally called nextToken() on the delegate parser upon switching, which incorrectly advanced the parser past the current token if it was already positioned on one.
prediction: The fix should introduce a mechanism to check if the delegate already has a current token and return it instead of calling nextToken() again, or suppress the initial call to nextToken() when switching.
experiment_rationale: The diff shows the introduction of a _suppressNextToken flag and a modification to the nextToken() logic to check for existing tokens (delegate.hasCurrentToken()) before calling nextToken(), confirming the procedural logic for token retrieval was flawed.
reasoning_summary: The defect is in the procedural logic of how tokens are retrieved when switching between parsers in a sequence. It is not a missing guard (Checking) or a simple value assignment (Assignment/Initialization), but a fundamental flaw in the iteration/retrieval algorithm used to traverse the concatenated parsers.

alternative_types.why_not_primary: While the fix adds a check (hasCurrentToken), the core issue is the incorrect algorithmic strategy for advancing the parser, not just a missing validation guard.
